text.title: Jobs to Be Done (JTBD) Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Jobs to Be Done (JTBD) application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every job MUST be stated in the exact pattern: "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome]." Missing clauses are rejected.
rule.rationale: The pattern forces situation + motivation + outcome to be separated; one missing clause hides the actual job.

rule.statement: Every job MUST carry functional, emotional, and social dimensions. Job statements with only the functional dimension are rejected.
rule.rationale: Functional-only jobs miss the emotional and social pressures that actually trigger switching; positioning copy then targets the wrong lever.

rule.statement: The artefact MUST name all four forces of progress per job: Push (current situation pain), Pull (new solution attraction), Anxiety (switching fear), Habit (status-quo inertia). All four are required, even if zero.
rule.rationale: Switching is a balance of forces; ignoring habit/anxiety produces marketing that pulls but does not overcome inertia.

rule.statement: Each job statement MUST cite ≥2 switch-interview transcripts as evidence. Statements without citations are rejected.
rule.rationale: Jobs reverse-engineered from analytics are guesses; switch interviews are the falsifiability anchor.

rule.statement: The deep variant MUST include the 8-stage job map (define, locate, prepare, confirm, execute, monitor, modify, conclude) with breakdown points named per stage.
rule.rationale: The stage map is the discovery instrument; without it the artefact regresses to a job statement, not a job analysis.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
